coredata.dc:title: Aggregated gold nanoparticles rich in electromagnetic field “hotspots” for surface enhanced Raman scattering
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yongcong
Zhang, Jingwen
Lai, Xiaojing
Chen, Yiquan
Fu, Fengfu
Lin, Zhenyu
Dong, Yongqiang
coredata.dc:description: A simple method for one-step synthesis of aggregated gold nanoparticles (a-AuNPs) using single-layer carbon dots (s-CDs) as the capping agents has been proposed. The obtained a-AuNPs are mainly composed of several spherical AuNPs of 20–25 nm sized, which aggregate to form nanogaps of ∼1 nm. Furthermore, the obtained a-AuNPs produce a strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption band centered at around 640 nm, which is quite close to the wavelength of the commonly used 633 nm laser in surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS). Thus, under the irradiation of 633 nm laser, a lot of electromagnetic field “hot spots” are formed at around the nanogaps, and strong SERS activity is achieved. The obtained a-AuNPs are dropped on tin-foil wafers to fabricate SERS substrates, which show the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, good repeatability and satisfactory stability. On the basis, a sensitive SERS sensor is developed to detect malachite green in aquaculture water, with a low detection limit of 1 × 10−9 mol/L.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregated gold nanoparticles
Plasmonic hotspots
Localized surface plasmon resonance
Surface enhanced Raman scattering
Malachite green

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), HRTEM image (b), XRD pattern (c), DLS spectrum (d) and XPS spectrum (e) of a-AuNPs, high resolution XPS spectra of Au4f (f) and C1s (g) of a-AuNP, FITR spectra of CDs and a-AuNPs (h), photographs (i) and the corresponding UV–vis spectra (j) of the products synthesized at different pH conditions.

SERS spectra of 1 × 10−7 mol/L on the substrates obtained by loading different amount of a-AuNPs on 5 × 5 cm2 tin-foil wafers (a). Effect of loading amount of a-AuNPs on the SERS intensity of CV at 1618 cm−1 (b).

SERS spectra of CV of various concentrations (a), the plot of SERS intensity at 1618 cm−1 as a function of the logarithmic CV concentration (b), SERS spectra of CV collected from 50 points on one of the substrate (c), SERS intensity distribution of CV at 1618 cm−1 based on the 50 SERS spectra collected from one substrate (d), SERS intensity distribution of CV at 1618 cm−1 based on and five substrates fabricated by different batches a-AuNPs (e), SERS spectrum of CV on the substrate with different storage times (f), effect of storage time on the SERS intensity of CV at 1618 cm−1 (g). The concentration of CV in (c)–(g) is 1 × 10−7 mol/L.
SERS spectra of MG of various concentrations (a), the plot of SERS intensity at 1618 cm−1 as a function of the logarithmic MG concentration (b).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of a-AuNPs.
The main characteristic Raman peaks of CV on the substate and the corresponding band assignment [39].

row.entry: Ring skeletal vibration of radical orientation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of recovery experiments for detecting MG using the SERS sensor.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a n = 3, each spiked concentration was obtained from three parallel experiments.

head.ce:title: Aggregated gold nanoparticles rich in electromagnetic field “hotspots” for surface enhanced Raman scattering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350108, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple method for one-step synthesis of aggregated gold nanoparticles (a-AuNPs) using single-layer carbon dots (s-CDs) as the capping agents has been proposed. The obtained a-AuNPs are mainly composed of several spherical AuNPs of 20–25 nm sized, which aggregate to form nanogaps of ∼1 nm. Furthermore, the obtained a-AuNPs produce a strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption band centered at around 640 nm, which is quite close to the wavelength of the commonly used 633 nm laser in surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS). Thus, under the irradiation of 633 nm laser, a lot of electromagnetic field “hot spots” are formed at around the nanogaps, and strong SERS activity is achieved. The obtained a-AuNPs are dropped on tin-foil wafers to fabricate SERS substrates, which show the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, good repeatability and satisfactory stability. On the basis, a sensitive SERS sensor is developed to detect malachite green in aquaculture water, with a low detection limit of 1 × 10−9 mol/L.

A simple method is developed for one-step synthesis of aggregated gold nanoparticles showing strong localized surface plasmon resonance absorption at around 640 nm, which are applied as excellent surface enhanced Raman scattering substrates.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is a developing spectroscopic analysis technique. It has the advantages of fast response, sensitive detection, high-throughput analysis, and can provide fingerprint spectra of the tested molecules [1]. Therefore, SERS is considered a very promising analytical technique with outstanding application potential in many fields such as food safety [2], biopharmaceutical analysis [3], public safety [4], and environmental monitoring [5].

SERS requires the target molecule closed to the surface of certain special nanomaterials (substrates), which can provide electromagnetic enhancement (EM) or/and chemical enhancement (CM) effects to enhance greatly the weak Raman signal of the target molecule [6,7]. In recent decades, extensive research has been conducted on the mechanism of SERS, and various novel nanomaterials/structures have been continuously constructed to serve as SERS substrates based on the proposed mechanisms [6–22]. The commonly used SERS substrates include coinage-metal nanostructures [6–11], semiconductor nanomaterials [12–15], carbon nanomaterials [16,17], MXenes nanomaterials [18–20], and hybrid nanomaterials of coinage-metal nanostructures with semiconductor nanomaterials [21,22], etc. Among these SERS substrates, Au based nanostructures are undoubtedly the most popular in practical applications. Firstly, Au based nanostructures typically exhibit strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption, thus may generate electromagnetic field “hot spots” at the contained nanogaps under the irradiation of corresponding lasers, resulting in excellent EM effect [23,24]. Secondly, the Fermi level of Au also provides possibilities for many target molecules to produce charge transfer (CT) under the irradiation of lasers, gaining an undeniable CM effect [23,24]. Therefore, Au based substrates usually exhibit satisfactory SERS activity towards many target molecules. Many interesting Au based nanostructures have been constructed for SERS substrates, such as nanocones [8], nanostars [9], nanoplates [10,25], triangulars [26], nanowires [27], nanoparticles [28], and triangular nanoprisms [29], et, al. Unfortunately, those substrates often have some drawbacks such as complex preparation processes [8,10,26,30], or a lack of electromagnetic field “hot spots” [9,24,27–29]. Developing simple method to prepare Au nanostructure rich in electromagnetic field “hot spots” is still necessary. Aggregated AuNPs (a-AuNPs) usually possess a large quantity of nanogaps, which may turn to electromagnetic field “hot spots”. However, the a-AuNPs obtained by reported methods are often difficult to maintain stability and ensure LSPR wavelength matching with lasers, thus making it difficult to gain strong and stable SERS activity. Herein, a novel and simple method is developed to synthesize a-AuNPs, using single-layer carbon dots as special capping and bridging agents [31,32]. The obtained a-AuNPs show stable dispersion in aqueous solution and a strong LSPR absorption matching with the 633 nm laser, therefore show a good SERS activity that possess a high enhancement factor (EF) of 3.0 × 106 towards 1 × 10−8 mol/L crystal violent (CV). On the basis, a sensitive SERS sensor is constructed for the detection of malachite green (MG). The constructed sensor shows the advantages of low detection limit (1 × 10−9 mol/L), wide response range (1 × 10−9 ∼1 × 10−7 mol/L), fast response, good repeatability (low relative standard deviation values of 6.01 % and 6.58 % from one SERS substrate and five substrates prepared by different batches of a-AuNPs) and satisfactory stability (15 days), and can be applied in actual aquaculture water samples.

Chemicals: The chemicals including chloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 99.9 %), (+)-D-glucose (99.0 %), CV (AR), MG (95 %) were purchased from Macklin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Nitric acid (HNO3), ethanol, acetone and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were of analytical grade, and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water (DI water, 18.2 MΩ cm) from a Thermo Scientific Nanopure system was used throughout the experiment.

Synthesis of a-AuNPs: a-AuNPs were prepared by a one-step reduction and self-assembly method, using single-layer carbon dots (s-CDs) as the capping agents (Scheme 1 ). The used s-CDs were mainly of 3–8 nm lateral sizes and 0.4–1.0 nm heights, obtained using a classical chemical oxidation method from XC-72 carbon black [31]. The abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and unique structure make the s-CDs as ideal capping and bridging agents to prepare aggregated metal nanoparticles [31,32]. The typical operation of a-AuNPs was as follows: 23 mL of water was added into an 80 mL beaker, then 1 mL of s-CDs solution (5 mg/mL) and 250 μL of HAuCl4 solution (0.05 mol/L) were added under stirring. Subsequently, ∼1.7 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (0.1 mol/L) was added into the solution to adjust the pH value to around 8.5. As soon as the solution was heated to boiling, 800 μL of glucose solution (0.1 mol/L) was added into the solution quickly. Within ∼5 min, the color of the solution turned from yellow to orange and finally to blue, suggesting the formation of a-AuNPs. Then, the heating was stopped while the stirring was continued, allowing the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. Finally, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove the supernatant. The obtained precipitate was re-dispersed in DI water to obtain a dispersion of a-AuNPs, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C before using.

Instruments and Characterizations: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of AuNPs and a-AuNPs, and the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of a-AuNPs were measured using a HRTEM JEOL 2100 system operating at 200 kV. Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) image of a-AuNPs on the tin-foil wafer was measured using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Sigma 300). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of a-AuNPs was obtained from a Rigaku D/max-3C (Japan) using Cu Ka radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of single-layer CDs and CDs/AuNPs were measured using an ESCAlab 250 XPS system having an Al K source. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of s-CDs and a-AuNPs were measured using a FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of the a-AuNPs was carried out using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS laser particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). UV/Vis spectra of s-CDs and a-AuNPs were obtained from a UV/Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950) with a scan range of 200–900 nm. SERS and Raman spectra were measured by a Renishaw 1000 microspectrometer equipped with a 633 nm laser. The laser power is ∼0.6 mW, the area of laser spot is ∼1.25 μm2, and the accumulation time was 10 s. The objective lens of the microscope is 50 × magnification, with a numerical aperture of 0.5.

SERS Measurement: Fresh tin-foil was cut into small pieces of 5 mm × 5 mm sized. The tin-foil wafers were put into a clean beaker, and was ultrasonically cleaned using ethanol, acetone, and DI water in sequence. Then, 20 μL of a-AuNPs was dropped evenly onto a clean tin-foil wafer and dried at 40 °C to obtain a SERS substrate. For the SERS detection, 20 μL of standard solution or actual sample was dropped evenly on the substrate, and dried at 40 °C. It should be noted that during the substrate preparation process, the tin-foil wafers should be kept as flat as possible to reduce the signal deviation caused by unstable laser focusing plane during the following SERS detection.

Two important characteristics of the used s-CDs, namely small size and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, make them to be ideal capping agents in preparing various of nanomaterials [21,32,33]. The TEM image shown in Fig. 1 a suggests that the obtained a-AuNPs are mainly composed of several spherical AuNPs. The diameter of a single AuNPs is mainly around 20–25 nm, while the size of a-AuNPs is around 60–110 nm. In fact, the obtained a-AuNPs are mainly composed of 3–5 AuNPs and 2–7 nanogaps. The HRTEM image shown in Fig. 1b indicates that the gaps among the AuNPs are only ∼1 nm, which are usually electromagnetic field “hot spots” [23,34]. Clear lattice fringes can also be observed from the HRTEM image, suggesting the good crystallinity of the obtained AuNPs. The XRD pattern of the obtained a-AuNPs shows four main diffraction peaks at 38.28°, 44.41°, 64.70° and 77.70° (Fig. 1c), corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) reflections of face centered cubic (fcc) of gold, respectively. The result also confirms the good crystallinity of the gold nanostructures contained in the obtained materials. The DLS spectrum of the obtained a-AuNPs shows three particle size distribution peaks, which are around 4 nm, 20 nm, and 90 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d). The peak at around 4 nm should be related to S-CDs, while the peaks at around 20 nm and 90 nm correspond to the size of AuNPs and a-AuNPs, respectively. XPS spectrum reveals that the obtained a-AuNPs are mainly composed of Au, C and O elements. (Fig. 1e). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Au4f exhibits two binding energy peaks at 83.68 and 87.38 eV, corresponding to the metallic state of Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2, respectively (Fig. 1f). There are no other binding energy peaks, indicating that the Au element in the obtained material has been completely reduced to metallic Au. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s mainly includes 5 binding energy peaks (Fig. 1g), corresponding to Sp2 C–C (284.5 eV), Sp3 C–C (285.3 eV), C–OH (286.3 eV), CO (287.2 eV), and OC–OH (288.5 eV), respectively. The peaks agree well with those of s-CDs [31]. suggesting that the C element in the obtained a-AuNPs should be mainly resulted from the s-CDs capped on the AuNPs. The FTIR spectrum of a-AuNPs only shows the characteristic absorption peaks of s-CDs [35,36], including functional groups such as O–H, C–H, CO, CC, C–O, etc. (Fig. 1h). The result is consistent with the XPS analysis, proving that a large amount of s-CDs are encapsulated on the surface of the obtained a-AuNPs. The large number of oxygen-containing functional groups carried by the capped s-CDs make the obtained a-AuNPs rich in negative charges, as confirmed by the Zeta potential measurement (−35 mV). Therefore, the obtained a-AuNPs can be uniformly and stably dispersed in aqueous solution during a one-month observation. As shown in Fig. 1i, the obtained dispersion of a-AuNPs in DI water appears clear blue, and the corresponding UV–vis extinction spectrum exhibits two characteristic LSPR peaks located at around 525 nm and 640 nm, respectively (Fig. 1j). The peak at 525 nm is determined by the sizes of AuNPs, while the other peak at 640 nm is dependent on the sizes of a-AuNPs.

All the above characterization results demonstrate that s-CDs can be used as capping agents to prepare a-AuNPs. It should be pointed out that s-CDs have been used as capping agent to synthesize AuNPs in other works [21,33,37,38]. However, unlike this work, the obtained AuNPs were all monodispersed. The main reason for this significant difference should be related to the control of pH value of the reaction solution. As shown in Fig. 1i and j, under acidic conditions, glucose cannot reduce AuCl4 −, so almost no metallic Au is generated. Under both neutral and alkaline conditions, glucose can reduce AuCl4 − into AuNPs. However, under neutral condition (pH = 7), the obtained AuNPs are mainly monodispersed, whose dispersion in water exhibits the characteristic violet-red color. The corresponding UV–vis spectrum shows only one LSPR absorption peak around 546 nm. TEM imaging also confirms that the obtained AuNPs are mainly monodispersed, with particle sizes mainly distributed in the range of 25–35 nm (Fig. S1). All the characterizations agree well with those reported in our previous works [21,33]. Under alkaline conditions, the obtained materials are a-AuNPs rather than monodispersed AuNPs. However, under strong alkaline conditions, such as at a pH of 10, the aggregation degree of the obtained a-AuNPs is relatively uneven, resulting in a broadening and weakening of the LSPR absorption. Only under weakly alkaline conditions, such as at a pH of 8.5, the obtained a-AuNPs achieve strong and sharp LSPR absorption.

Firstly, the SERS activity of the constructed substrates was evaluated. Fig. 3 a shows the SERS spectra of CV of various concentrations on the substrate. It can be seen that the constructed SERS substrate has a sensitive response to CV. The main characteristic Raman peaks of CV are still clear and distinguishable even at a low concentration of 5 × 10−9 mol/L. Furthermore, in a wide concentration range from 5 × 10−9 to 1 × 10−6 mol/L, there is a significant positive correlation between signal intensity and concentration. The characteristic peak intensity at 1618 cm−1 shows a good semi-logarithmic relationship with the concentration of CV (Fig. 3b). Comparing the SERS intensity of 1 × 10−8 mol/L at 1618 cm−1 CV with the Raman signal of CV powder (Fig. S3), the EF of the substrate towards CV can be calculated to be about 3.0 × 106 (see the calculation detail from SI). This value is comparable to those of most Au based SERS substrates (Table S1). Apparently, although the synthesis method is much simpler than those of other Au based SERS substrates, the constructed substrates based on the obtained a-AuNPs show satisfactory SERS activity. Secondly, the signal uniformity of the SERS substrate was investigated. Fig. 3c shows the SERS spectra collected randomly from 50 points of one piece of substrate. It can be seen that there is no significant difference across the 50 spectra. The characteristic peak intensity at 1618 cm−1 is mainly distributed in the range of 51000∼64000, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 6.01 % (Fig. 3d). It suggests that the constructed SERS substrate has good signal uniformity, which should be due to the relatively uniform distribution of a-AuNPs on the tin-foil wafer. Thirdly, the reproducibility of substrates was examined by measuring the SERS spectrum of CV from five substrates constructed using different batches of a-AuNPs. It is found that the characteristic peak intensity of CV at 1618 cm−1 is also generally distributed in the range of 51000∼65000, with a low RSD value of 6.58 % (Fig. 3e). This result indicates that there is no significant difference between the SERS substrates prepared from different batches of synthesized a-AuNPs. Finally, the stability of the constructed SERS substrates was also evaluated. Fig. 3f shows the SERS spectra of 1 × 10−7 mol/L CV on the substrates with different storage times. During the 15 days’ observation (tested every three days), the SERS spectrum of CV has nearly no change, and the characteristic peak intensity at 1618 cm−1 shows no significant decrease (Fig. 3g). The results indicate that the constructed SERS substrates can maintain high activity during long-term storage, which should be mainly due to the excellent chemical stability of C and Au.

The above experimental results indicate that the constructed SERS substrates have outstanding advantages such as high activity, good signal uniformity, good reproducibility and long-term stability, which make them have good application prospects in sensing. To validate it, the constructed SERS substrates were further applied to develop a SERS sensor for MG in environmental water. MG is a dark green crystalline solid triarylmethane dye, which is widely used as a fungicide and anti-protozoan agent in the aquaculture industry. Due to the long residual time and high toxicity of MG, even trace intake may accumulate and cause health problems [40]. MG has been listed as one of the prohibited veterinary drugs, and is subject to strict legal supervision in multiple countries and regions. However, due to the increasing consumption of aquaculture products, the high bactericidal effect, low cost, and lack of effective alternatives, MG has never been completely eradicated. Therefore, monitoring MG in aquaculture water is still very necessary.

Fig. 4 a shows the SERS spectra of a series of MG standard solutions with different concentrations on the constructed substrates. It can be seen that MG has good SERS response on the substrate. It can be seen that the SERS substrate has sensitive response to MG. The main characteristic peaks of MG are still clearly distinguishable even at a low concentration of 1 × 10−9 mol/L (0.365 ppb), which is much lower than the maximum residue limits (2 ppb) [41,42]. Therefore, the constructed SERS sensor has sufficient sensitivity to meet the needs of detection. Furthermore, the SERS intensity of MG shows a significant positive correlation with the concentration of MG. Typically, the characteristic peak intensity at 1618 cm−1 exhibits a typical semi-logarithmic relationship with the concentration in the range from 1 × 10−9 to 1 × 10−7 mol/L (Fig. 4b). Thus, the quantitative detection of MG can be achieved. It should be pointed out that the main characteristic peaks of MG are almost identical with those of CV due to the similar molecular structural formulas (Fig. S4). The only obvious difference is that CV has a very weak C–H rocking peak at 1221 cm−1, with a signal intensity of less than 3 % of the strongest peak intensity at 1618 cm−1, while the MG has a very obvious C–H rocking peak at 1221 cm−1, with an intensity of about 22 % of its peak intensity at 1618 cm−1. Thus, the intensity ratio of the two peaks can serve as an important parameter for distinguishing between CV and MG.

To verify the reliability of the constructed SERS sensor, a local aquaculture water sample was collected to detect. The experimental results indicate that the collected aquaculture water sample produce no distinguishable signal of MG (Fig. S5), indicating that the MG content in the water sample is lower than the detection limit of the sensing system. Therefore, the water sample was spiked to different concentrations for detection (Fig. S5). As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of the spiked samples are mainly distributed in 91∼106 %. The satisfactory recoveries and low RSD suggest that the SERS substrates constructed by the a-AuNPs are great promising for quantitative analysis in actual environmental water samples.

A simple method has been developed to synthesize a-AuNPs, which possess a strong LSPR absorption centered at around 640 nm. Under the irradiation of a 633 nm laser, the abundant electromagnetic field “hot spots” formed at the nanogaps contained in the a-AuNPs gave them strong SERS activity. The obtained a-AuNPs were further dropped on tin-foil wafers to fabricate SERS substrates. On the basis, a sensitive SERS sensor is developed to detect MG, with a low detection limit of 1 × 10−9 mol/L. The practicality of the sensor was proved by the satisfactory results of detecting MG in an actual aquaculture water sample.

Yongcong Zhou: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Jingwen Zhang: Methodology, Data curation. Xiaojing Lai: Formal analysis, Data curation. Yiquan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Fengfu Fu: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhenyu Lin: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yongqiang Dong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22276032), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2022J01537, 2023N0030, 2022Y0059, 2021Y0006), the Fujian Provincial Department of Education - Fujian Province Young and Middle-aged Teacher Education Research Project, Project number (JAT220003), and Fuzhou University Valuable Instruments and Equipment Open Test Fund, Project number (2023T010).
